pair.title: Show HN: Prompt-to-Excalidraw demo with Gemma 4 E2B in the browser (3.1GB)

story.title: Prompt → Diagram — Gemma 4 E2B in desktop Chrome (WebGPU)

comments.text: Ran this in Chrome 134 on an M1 MacBook Pro. Model loads in about 8 seconds, then generates a flowchart from "user login flow with password reset" in roughly 3 seconds flat. The compressed KV cache actually works-I tested with a 15-turn conversation and didn't hit memory pressure until turn 18. Output code is genuinely compact (saw 47 tokens for a moderately complex state diagram). WebGPU subgroups requirement is a real blocker for most people though. Would be nice to see a CPU fallback for older Chrome versions, even if

kids.text:
This is exactly the kind of constraint-driven optimization that makes demos actually useful. The 2.4x KV cache compression via WGSL is clever-polar quantization + QJL sidesteps the usual trade-off between context length and memory footprint.

The 30+ tok/s on M1 is solid too. I was skeptical about WebGPU subgroups being viable for inference until I saw the actual numbers here. Three seconds for a 15-turn conversation to diagram is fast enough that it feels interactive, which matters more than raw benchmark numbers.

The real win is that this ships entirely client-side. No inference API, no quota limits, no latency hiding. You can run it offline (after the initial model download).

The WebGPU subgroups requirement does cut the audience down though-basically Chrome desktop only right now. Would be interesting to see if the WASM+SIMD fallback

Tested on Chrome 134 / M2 MacBook. Model load 9s, then I ran the same 15-turn test. Hit memory pressure at turn 19, not 18 (might be variance in garbage collection). tok/s stayed above 28 throughout. The polar quant

The WebGPU subgroups gate is real, but it's worth noting that the polar quantization itself (independent of the shader implementation) works fine on CPU. The turboquant-wasm package linked in the article handles this, you lose the 30+ tok/s on GPU, but you

The CPU fallback angle is worth exploring further. The turboquant-wasm package trades throughput for compatibility, but the real bottleneck isn't just speed-it's the quantization scheme itself. Polar coordinates + QJL are algorithmic wins independent of the hardware.

If someone backported the polar quantization logic to run on older GPUs (say, ones without subgroups support), you'd still get maybe 15-20 tok/s on a decent discrete card, which beats CPU SIMD handily. The shader implementation is clever because it parallelizes the KV cache compression pass itself, not just the forward pass.

The 2.4x compression ratio is the real story here. That's not a tiny win-it's the difference between

comments.text: This is just a demo, not a real product. Running a 27B model in the browser at 30 tok/s sounds fake. That's either compressed to uselessness or they're cherry-picking the fastest sequences. Where's the actual latency on a real prompt, not "user login flow"?

kids.text:
I actually ran this locally and got 28 tok/s on my M1 MacBook. The latency on a real prompt (I tried a 200-token diagram spec) was around 7 seconds end-to-end, which includes the model load and shader compilation. Not cherry-picked-that's consistent across multiple runs.

The KV cache compression is the key insight here. TurboQuant via WGSL is legit clever, though you're right to be skeptical about the "no server" claim mattering much if it only works in Chrome 134+. Still, the fact that a 27B model

comments.text:
The KV cache compression via WGSL is the interesting bit here, but I'd want to see the actual memory overhead numbers. WGSL compute shaders have their own alignment requirements and register pressure, does the 2.4x figure account for GPU-side overhead, or is that just the theoretical token savings?

Also, the polar + QJL quantization scheme works well for dense models like Gemma, but I'm curious how it degrades on the edges. Did you run perplexity evals on the compressed vs. uncompressed outputs? And what's the latency breakdown, is the 30 tok/s dominated by the compute shader dispatch, or is it the model inference itself?

The bigger win here is shipping this without a server or API key. That's genuinely useful for privacy-sensitive diagram generation. But the WebGPU subgroups requirement is a hard ceiling for now (Safari support is still in progress

kids.text: The 2.4x is the compressed token representation size, not GPU memory. The actual GPU overhead is higher because of WGSL alignment (you're packing into vec4 slots), so real VRAM savings are closer to 1.8–2.0x after accounting for shader scratch

This reminds me of the old WebGL compute shader hacks people were doing circa 2015 for physics sims. The alignment issue you're hitting (vec4 packing) is exactly why everyone eventually moved to Vulkan compute for serious work. Have you considered using shared memory barriers to reduce global

comments.text: Stop thinking of this as a browser demo and think of it as WebGPU finally being useful for something beyond benchmarks. Yann LeCun's been saying for years that inference needs to move client-side for privacy, and this is the first time I've seen it actually work without JavaScript jank

kids.text: Check out https://github.com/mlc-ai/web-llm, they've been shipping browser inference for 18 months now, handles quantization client-side without the shader complexity. Different architecture but solves the same privacy problem. How does the KV cache compression compare to their approach?

I ran the web-llm demo on my M1 Mac last week with a similar workload (long conversation, 8K context). Hit OOM after about 15 messages. Tried their quantization flags and it helped, but still capped out way earlier than this TurboQuant approach claims. Just loaded the Excalid

Ran this locally on Chrome 134 (M1 MacBook, 16GB RAM). Got 28 tok/s on the first prompt, then 34 tok/s after the KV cache kicked in. The Excalidraw output was legible but the flowchart boxes were slightly misaligned on the second diagram, probably a tokenization edge case. Still, this is the first time I

comments.text: Tested this on a 2020 M1 Air and got similar perf. The WGSL shader optimization is clever, but what really surprised me was how snappy the Excalidraw output felt. Generated a swimlane diagram in under 2 seconds on the second run.

kids.text:
Thanks for testing on M1. Yeah, the second-run snappiness is the KV cache compression kicking in, after the initial prefill, you're working with ~2.4x less data per attention pass, so the GPU stays fed without cache thrashing.

The Excalidraw code generation is doing most of the heavy lifting though. The model just needs to emit ~50 tokens of structured output instead of the full 5k JSON blob. We're basically using quantization + aggressive tokenization to make the whole thing fit in 3GB.

One limitation we're hitting: longer conversations (20+ turns) start spilling past the 3GB budget even with TurboQuant. I'm looking at either dropping to f32 for the weight matrices (trades inference speed for capacity) or implementing a sliding window KV cache for the next iteration. Would be curious if you hit that ceiling in any of your tests.

The second-run speedup is mostly just model warm-up in the shader compiler. First generation often hits GPU cache misses because the WGSL pipeline isn't fully optimized until the first batch completes. Real bottleneck on M1 is memory bandwidth to GPU, not compute-30 tok/s is hitting that ceiling pretty hard. Still impressive for browser-side though. Have you tested what happens with longer context? KV cache compression usually has quality tradeoffs

Have you looked at the turboquant-wasm package on npm? It does the same polar + QJL compression on CPU via SIMD, which actually outperforms the shader version on some M-series chips depending on your memory layout. The tradeoff is you lose the GPU warmup benefits but gain more predictable latency. We've been using it for a context-heavy chat UI and it handles 8K token contexts without the quality loss you'd expect from 2.4x compression.
